skill.name: browser-testing-with-screenshots
skill.description: Automates Chrome browser testing with visual verification, element selection, page inspection, and local screenshot evidence for web application workflows.
skill.summary: Test web application flows in Chrome and verify results with screenshots.
skill.author: AgentWorkforce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many command examples, network URLs, and browser screenshot references, but I found no evidence of malware, hidden upload, prompt injection, or weak cryptography. The main risk is legitimate but sensitive browser automation: the skill can run external tools, control Chrome, evaluate page JavaScript, capture screenshots, and access authenticated session state.

medium_findings.title: External Browser Toolchain Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill requires installing agent-tools from GitHub and running browser-* executables from PATH. This is expected for the skill, but it creates supply-chain and command execution risk if the external toolchain or PATH is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is explicit and central to the skill. The intent appears legitimate, but the dependency and PATH execution model require user trust.

medium_findings.title: Browser Session Data Exposure Risk
medium_findings.description: The skill documents browser cookie access, content extraction, screenshot capture, and state preservation. When used with a real browser profile, these actions can expose authenticated page content or session data in local output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly describes cookies, content extraction, screenshots, and preserved login state. No upload is shown, so the risk is exposure through local automation artifacts.

medium_findings.title: Page Context JavaScript Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill uses browser-eval.js to execute JavaScript in the active page. This is useful for testing, but it can read or modify DOM state and form values if pointed at sensitive pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The JavaScript execution examples are concrete and repeated. They appear intended for localhost testing, which lowers but does not remove the risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are an installation link and localhost navigation examples. I found no evidence that the skill sends data to an external collection endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible examples and local development addresses. There is no hidden network transmission logic in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Screen Capture Upload Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static screen capture warnings are false positives. The file describes taking screenshots and saving local evidence, but I found no upload instruction or remote destination for screenshots.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Screenshot capture is explicitly local and user-visible. No evidence found for upload behavior in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flags appear to be keyword false positives. I found no cryptographic algorithm use, key handling, hashing workflow, or encryption code in the cited skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe the skill, text checks, and navigation checks. No evidence found for weak cryptographic behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Chrome Remote Debugging Control
dangerous_patterns.description: browser-start.js launches Chrome with debugging enabled, which allows automation control through Chrome DevTools Protocol.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly starts Chrome with remote debugging. This is necessary for the workflow but increases the impact of untrusted automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie and Session Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: browser-cookies.js is listed as a supported command, and the skill recommends preserving browser state for login sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability is documented directly. The risk depends on whether the user tests with sensitive authenticated sessions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Active Page JavaScript Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: browser-eval.js examples execute JavaScript against the current page and can inspect page content, forms, selectors, and location state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly evaluate JavaScript in the page context. The behavior is legitimate for testing but sensitive by nature.

content.user_title: Test Web UIs with Visual Browser Evidence
content.value_statement: Manual UI checks can miss visual regressions and take repeated effort. This skill guides browser automation with screenshots, element selection, and page inspection for verifiable web testing.
actual_capabilities: Starts Chrome with remote debugging for browser automation workflows.
Navigates to local web application pages and waits for visual states.
Captures local screenshots to document UI behavior and regressions.
Uses interactive element picking to find selectors for dynamic interfaces.
Runs page JavaScript checks through browser-eval.js for DOM and state verification.
Extracts readable page content for comparison with expected UI text.
limitations: Requires the external agent-tools browser utilities to be installed and trusted.
Best suited for development and localhost testing, not untrusted public websites.
Screenshots and extracted content may include sensitive data from active sessions.
Does not replace full test frameworks, assertions, or cross-browser coverage.

use_cases.title: Verify Frontend Changes
use_cases.description: Check a changed page in Chrome, capture screenshots, and confirm that key UI elements render correctly.

use_cases.title: Collect QA Evidence
use_cases.description: Run guided browser flows and save visual proof for login, dashboard, profile, or other user journeys.

use_cases.title: Debug Dynamic Selectors
use_cases.description: Use interactive picking and page JavaScript checks to inspect complex interfaces during development.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Baseline Screenshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my local app in Chrome, navigate to the dashboard page, and capture a baseline screenshot for review.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Login Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser testing to open the login page, fill the test credentials, submit the form, and capture screenshots before and after login.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Dynamic Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the element picker and page evaluation to verify that the dynamic navigation menu appears and responds after interaction.

prompt_templates.title: Create Visual Test Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a visual pass across login, dashboard, and profile pages, save organized screenshots, and summarize any layout or content issues.

output_examples.input: Test the dashboard after a frontend change.
output_examples.output: A short report listing the visited page, screenshot path, visible dashboard sections, and any unexpected visual issues.

output_examples.input: Verify that the login form works visually.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step result with screenshots for the empty form, filled form, and post-login page state.

output_examples.input: Check whether a dynamic menu is selectable.
output_examples.output: A concise finding that names the selected element, confirms interaction behavior, and notes any selector fragility.

best_practices: Use a clean browser profile when testing pages that may contain sensitive accounts or cookies.
Store screenshots in a dedicated test-results directory and review them before sharing.
Prefer localhost and staging targets that contain test data rather than production user data.
anti_patterns: Do not run browser-eval.js against untrusted pages with sensitive authenticated sessions.
Do not publish screenshots before checking for credentials, tokens, or private user data.
Do not rely on visual screenshots alone when automated assertions are required.

faq.question: Does this skill upload screenshots?
faq.answer: No evidence found for upload behavior. The documented workflow saves screenshots locally.

faq.question: What external tools does it need?
faq.answer: It depends on agent-tools from GitHub and the browser-* commands provided by that project.

faq.question: Can it access cookies?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill lists a cookie viewing command and discusses preserving login state between commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for production sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. It is better for localhost or staging environments with test accounts and non-sensitive data.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript in a page?
faq.answer: Yes. browser-eval.js examples run JavaScript in the active page context for testing and inspection.

faq.question: Is this a full testing framework?
faq.answer: No. It is a browser automation guide for visual checks and evidence collection.
